name: Noro Kureyon
best_for: Colorful scarves and blankets with zero color planning
expert_note: The yarn that made Noro famous. Each skein is a one-of-a-kind color journey. No two projects look alike.

name: Noro Silk Garden
best_for: Elegant shawls and wraps with natural color transitions
expert_note: Noro's flagship luxury yarn. The silk content gives a beautiful sheen that elevates simple stitch patterns.

name: Noro Iro
best_for: Sweaters and cardigans with dramatic color shifts
expert_note: Larger skeins mean longer color gradients. Perfect for garments where you want sweeping color changes.

name: Noro Taiyo
best_for: Spring and summer garments with Noro color magic
expert_note: Noro's answer to warm-weather knitting. The cotton-silk blend keeps the signature colors while adding breathability.

name: Noro Enka
best_for: Lightweight summer tops and market bags
expert_note: A quieter Noro with gentler color shifts. The high cotton content makes it ideal for warm-climate knitters.

name: Hamanaka Bonny
best_for: Amigurumi, kids' projects, and beginners on a budget
expert_note: Japan's go-to craft yarn. Every Japanese yarn shop stocks Bonny. The color consistency batch to batch is excellent.

name: Hamanaka Eco Andaria
best_for: Summer hats and structured bags with a natural look
expert_note: The original Japanese raffia-style yarn. Used for the iconic crocheted summer bags seen all over Tokyo in summer.

name: Hamanaka Amerry
best_for: Colorwork mittens and hats with crisp stitch patterns
expert_note: A workhorse merino at a fair price. The stitch definition makes it a favorite for Japanese colorwork patterns.

name: Hamanaka Wanpaku Denis
best_for: Children's garments and everyday items that need frequent washing
expert_note: Wanpaku means 'mischievous.' Designed for kids' wear that survives playground adventures and washing machines.

name: Daruma Iroiro
best_for: Fair Isle and colorwork with a massive color palette
expert_note: 72 colors of pure wool in a consistent weight. The go-to yarn for Japanese colorwork designers.

name: Daruma Genmou
best_for: Natural color projects and exploring different wool breeds
expert_note: Genmou means 'original wool.' Each color represents a different sheep breed's natural fleece tone.

name: Daruma Sashiko Thread
best_for: Sashiko embroidery and visible mending projects
expert_note: Daruma has been making sashiko thread since the Edo period. The twist is calibrated for running stitch work.

name: Daruma Classica
best_for: Simple elegant garments with excellent drape
expert_note: Daruma's answer to European merinos. Soft, well-spun, and available in timeless colors.

name: Richmore Percent
best_for: Japanese knitting pattern books requiring precise gauge
expert_note: The default yarn specified in many Japanese knitting pattern books. If a pattern says 'medium weight,' they often mean this.

name: Richmore Cashmere
best_for: Special occasion accessories and luxury gift knitting
expert_note: Richmore's premium line. The silk prevents pilling while letting the cashmere softness shine through.

name: Richmore Softy
best_for: Quick-knit cowls and chunky scarves for gifting
expert_note: The alpaca content gives it a halo effect without the price of pure alpaca. Great for last-minute gift knitting.

name: Diamond Masaki
best_for: Following Japanese knitting books with exact gauge match
expert_note: Another standard yarn for Japanese pattern books. When patterns call for DK merino, Masaki is usually the reference.

name: Diamond DiaKeito Mink
best_for: Statement scarves and collars with a fur-like texture
expert_note: A novelty yarn that produces a faux-fur effect with basic stockinette stitch. Very popular in Japanese fashion knitting.

name: Diamond DiaKeito Tess
best_for: Lace shawls and evening accessories with silk sheen
expert_note: Pure silk at a reasonable price point. The sheen makes lace patterns absolutely glow.

name: Diamond DiaKeito Diane
best_for: Cozy sweaters and accessories for sensitive skin
expert_note: Baby alpaca processed with Japanese precision. The heathered colorways blend beautifully in simple stitch patterns.

name: Olympus Emmy Grande
best_for: Crochet doilies, lace edging, and miniature amigurumi
expert_note: The gold standard for Japanese lace crochet. 80 colors means you can match any pattern book exactly.

name: Olympus Lace Thread No. 40
best_for: Intricate lace doilies and tatting projects
expert_note: No. 40 is the standard thickness for Japanese crochet lace books. Incredibly fine work is possible with this thread.

name: Olympus Make Make
best_for: Summer tops, baby items, and cotton washcloths
expert_note: A mid-weight cotton that works for both knitting and crochet. Softer than mercerized cottons.

name: Noro Silk Garden Sock
best_for: Statement socks and lightweight shawls with Noro colorways
expert_note: All the Noro color magic in a sock-friendly weight. The nylon content means these socks actually last.

name: Hamanaka Paume Baby Color
best_for: Baby clothes, blankets, and items for sensitive skin
expert_note: Hamanaka's organic line uses dyes certified safe for infant contact. The go-to for Japanese baby knitting.

name: Daruma Airy Wool Alpaca
best_for: Cozy accessories with a soft halo finish
expert_note: The alpaca gives a gentle fuzz around each stitch. Works beautifully for simple patterns that showcase the yarn.

name: Noro Kureyon Sock
best_for: Colorful socks and lightweight accessories with long color repeats
expert_note: 420 meters of Noro color magic. One skein makes a generous pair of socks with stunning self-striping.

name: Richmore Alpaca Regal
best_for: Warm winter accessories and outerwear
expert_note: 90% alpaca gives serious warmth. The small amount of wool adds elasticity that pure alpaca lacks.

name: Hamanaka Mohair Lino
best_for: Lightweight layered shawls and held-together sweaters
expert_note: Japanese mohair-silk at a fraction of European prices. Beautiful held together with a base yarn for textured sweaters.

name: Olympus Sashiko Thread
best_for: Sashiko embroidery and Japanese-style visible mending
expert_note: Olympus sashiko thread is slightly different in twist from Daruma, giving a different stitch appearance. Try both.

name: Diamond DiaKeito Mona Lisa
best_for: Everyday socks and accessories that need machine washing
expert_note: A practical blend that gives merino softness with acrylic durability. Great for gift knitting that recipients can machine wash.

name: Daruma Yokota Kogin Thread
best_for: Kogin-zashi geometric embroidery on even-weave fabric
expert_note: Kogin thread is thicker than sashiko thread, designed to fill the weave of fabric completely for geometric patterns.
